summary: Gruber dismantles Steve Ballmer's claims about Apple, iPods, and DRM by examining four quotes from a recent interview. He challenges Ballmer's assertion that most iPod music is stolen, arguing most users rip from legally purchased CDs. Gruber points out the fundamental contradiction in Microsoft's DRM strategy: making DRM 'easier' doesn't solve the problem because DRM's difficulty IS the point — it restricts what users want to do. He argues Apple is already leading the digital entertainment convergence precisely because it doesn't enforce draconian DRM, while Microsoft's corporate-market instincts are irrelevant in consumer electronics. The post draws historical parallels to Hollywood's failed opposition to the VCR to illustrate that consumer platforms succeed by pleasing consumers, not industry executives.
key_points: Ballmer's claim that most iPod music is stolen is likely wrong — most iPod owners have large CD collections they've legally ripped
The only thing 'hard' about DRM is when users want to do things DRM forbids, so making DRM 'easier' is a contradiction
Ballmer's own 12-year-old son instinctively understands the problem with DRM better than Microsoft's strategy acknowledges
The iPod's success on Windows already disproves Ballmer's claim that Apple can't achieve critical mass beyond the Mac
Microsoft's corporate-market instincts are a liability in consumer entertainment, where pleasing users matters more than pleasing executives
money_quotes: The most common format of music on an iPod is 'stolen.' — I'd love to see his source for this.
The only thing 'hard' about using DRM media is when you want to use it in ways that the DRM forbids.

Ballmer's 12-year-old son is, I'm guessing, smart enough to realize that there's no technical reason that would prevent him from putting 'all the music that he wants in all of the places that he would like it'.
If this is a joke, I get the feeling Apple will be laughing all the way to bank.
The iPod strikes me as existence proof that Apple can succeed outside the Mac user base.
Apple is already leading the way in terms of music — in large part because they don't enforce draconian DRM measures.
Successful consumer platforms are designed to make consumers happy, not clueless entertainment industry executives.
The film industry fought against the VCR, but it became wildly successful anyway, because consumers loved it.
key_insight: Consumer platforms win by delighting users, not by enforcing restrictions that please corporate executives — and Microsoft's instinct to serve the enterprise makes it fundamentally misread the consumer electronics market.
